one_line_takeaway: LSA-Probe scores membership in music diffusion models by the adversarial perturbation budget needed to destabilize the reverse-diffusion output past a perceptual-degradation threshold, beating loss/trajectory/SecMI baselines by 3-8 points of TPR@1%FPR under matched compute on DiffWave and MusicLDM, though absolute detection rates (13-20% TPR at 1% FPR, AUC 0.56-0.67) remain modest.
key_finding: Under matched compute (DDIM, tratio=0.6, p=2, eta_max=0.8, tau=P95), LSA-Probe improves the primary low-FPR endpoint (TPR@1% FPR) over the best of the three baselines in all four model/dataset combinations reported in Table 1: MusicLDM on MAESTRO 0.10 -> 0.13 (+0.03), MusicLDM on FMA-Large 0.08 -> 0.14 (+0.06), DiffWave on MAESTRO 0.12 -> 0.20 (+0.08), DiffWave on FMA-Large 0.11 -> 0.18 (+0.07). Corresponding AUC-ROC gains were +0.03 (MusicLDM, both datasets) and +0.04 (DiffWave, both datasets), with absolute AUC values only in the 0.56-0.67 range (i.e., attacks remain only modestly better than chance). Ablations (Fig. 2) show: peak separability at mid-trajectory timesteps (tratio=0.6); TPR@1% improves with larger perturbation budget eta until mild saturation near eta=0.6-0.8; and perceptual distances (CDPAM, MR-STFT) outperform training-aligned MSE metrics at low FPR. The paper also notes that even with LSA-Probe's gains, latent diffusion models (MusicLDM) remain more resistant to MIA than waveform DDPMs (DiffWave) across both datasets.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LSA-Probe (Latent Stability Adversarial Probe), a white-box membership inference attack (MIA) against music diffusion models. Instead of using loss/reconstruction-error signals, it measures a geometric property of the reverse diffusion trajectory: the minimal time-normalized adversarial perturbation budget (Cadv) needed, at an intermediate diffusion timestep t, to push the reverse-decoded output past a fixed perceptual degradation threshold tau (calibrated as the 95th percentile on a disjoint non-member development set). The core hypothesis is that training members sit in locally more stable ('flatter') regions of the generative manifold, so a larger adversarial budget is required to degrade them past the threshold; this budget itself becomes the membership score (higher Cadv => more likely a member). The method is a two-loop procedure: an outer bisection search (10 steps) over the perturbation budget eta, and an inner PGD loop (K=12 steps, momentum 0.9, restarts r=5) that finds the worst-case time-normalized perturbation delta_t = sigma_t * delta-tilde at a given eta, optimizing a differentiable perceptual distance D (primary: CDPAM; also reported: MR-STFT, log-mel MSE, waveform MSE) between the unperturbed and perturbed reverse-diffusion outputs. The authors state this is (to their knowledge) the first systematic investigation of MIA specifically for music diffusion models, and they connect local generative stability to membership via a first-order analysis that avoids needing likelihoods or shadow models. The method is derived for waveform DDPMs (DiffWave) and extended to latent diffusion (MusicLDM) by injecting the perturbation in VAE latent space and backpropagating through the frozen decoder.
threat_model: White-box, developer/auditor-side adversary: the attacker has read access to model parameters, the deterministic DDIM reverse sampler, and gradients with respect to inputs at intermediate diffusion states, but no shadow models, no training labels, and no access to likelihoods. The goal is a binary membership decision for a candidate waveform x0 against a single audited model. This models a forensic/copyright-auditing use case (e.g., a rights-holder or auditor with model access checking whether a specific music clip was used in training), not a purely black-box or API-only attacker.
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges/implies several limitations rather than claiming a solved problem: (1) absolute attack success is still low in the forensically relevant low-FPR regime -- best TPR@1% FPR values are 0.13-0.20, i.e., the attack detects at most a fifth of true members at a 1% false-positive budget, and AUC-ROC values (0.58-0.67) indicate only modest overall separability; (2) the white-box, gradient-access, developer-side threat model is restrictive and does not establish effectiveness against black-box/API-only deployments; (3) the attack is computationally expensive (~140 reverse diffusion passes per sample under the reported K=12, B=10 configuration), which the authors control for via matched-compute comparisons but which nonetheless represents a real per-sample cost; (4) results are reported on only two model families (DiffWave, MusicLDM) and two datasets (MAESTRO, FMA-Large), both music-domain corpora, so generalization to other audio domains (speech, sound effects) or to production commercial music-generation systems is untested; (5) the threshold tau=P95 is calibrated per experiment on a disjoint non-member development set, which requires the auditor to have such held-out non-member data available -- a nontrivial assumption for many audit scenarios; (6) no defenses or mitigations against LSA-Probe (e.g., differential privacy, regularization) are proposed or evaluated in this paper.

quantitative_results: MusicLDM/MAESTRO: TPR@1%FPR best baseline 0.10 (CI 0.07-0.12) -> LSA-Probe 0.13 (CI 0.10-0.15), delta +0.03; AUC 0.58+/-0.02 -> 0.61+/-0.03, delta +0.03
MusicLDM/FMA-Large: TPR@1%FPR best baseline 0.08 (CI 0.05-0.10) -> LSA-Probe 0.14 (CI 0.10-0.16), delta +0.06; AUC 0.56+/-0.01 -> 0.59+/-0.02, delta +0.03
DiffWave/MAESTRO: TPR@1%FPR best baseline 0.12 (CI 0.09-0.15) -> LSA-Probe 0.20 (CI 0.16-0.24), delta +0.08; AUC 0.63+/-0.02 -> 0.67+/-0.02, delta +0.04
DiffWave/FMA-Large: TPR@1%FPR best baseline 0.11 (CI 0.08-0.14) -> LSA-Probe 0.18 (CI 0.14-0.22), delta +0.07; AUC 0.62+/-0.02 -> 0.66+/-0.02, delta +0.04
Timestep ablation: peak separability at tratio=0.6 among {0.2,0.4,0.6,0.8}
Budget ablation: TPR@1% improves with eta until mild saturation near eta~0.6-0.8
Metric ablation: CDPAM and MR-STFT (perceptual metrics) outperform log-mel MSE and waveform MSE at low FPR
Compute: attack uses approximately (K+2)*B reverse passes and (K+1)*B metric evaluations per sample, with K=12, B=10 (~140 reverse passes); baselines matched within +/-5% compute
baselines_compared: Trajectory-reconstruction attack (PIA/PIAN, re-implemented from Kong et al. ICLR 2024)
